title: Matsvinskollen
subtitle: A three week concept project exploring how gamification could help people understand and reduce food waste.

intro.heading: Can interactive learning change how people think about food waste?
intro.body:
Matsvinskollen was a three week university project exploring how design could make food waste knowledge more practical and engaging.

Rather than building a finished product, we focused on understanding the problem and testing one clear idea. The result was a quiz based mobile concept designed to help users learn practical food storage and waste reduction tips through short interactive challenges.

role.heading: My role
role.body:
I worked across the full UX process, from early research to the final prototype.

My main contributions included conducting research, synthesising insights, designing the wireframes and high fidelity mobile interface, and building the interactive prototype in Figma. I also facilitated the user testing sessions and helped translate the findings into design improvements.

problem.heading: People know food waste is a problem but struggle to act on it
problem.body:
Most people are aware that food waste is an issue, but they often lack practical knowledge about how to reduce it in everyday life.

Our research showed that many people were unsure about how long food actually lasts, how to store it correctly, and which small decisions can prevent waste. Conversations around food waste often happened in social environments like school lunches, where group behaviour influences individual habits.

approach.heading: Research, synthesis, and rapid iteration
approach.body:
We started by exploring how people currently think and talk about food waste. From these insights we defined a focused problem that felt realistic within the three week timeframe.

Instead of designing a full product, we built a prototype around one core idea: using short gamified quizzes to make food waste knowledge easier to learn and remember.

outcome.heading: Shifting the conversation from abstract to practical
outcome.body:
During testing, users reported feeling more confident about food storage and food waste after completing the quizzes.

What stood out most was not how many questions they answered, but how their language changed. Participants began talking about food waste in more practical terms, focusing on storage, shelf life, and everyday decisions rather than the problem in general.

keyLearning.heading: A clear scope creates better insights
keyLearning.body:
With only three weeks available, trying to design a full solution would have limited what we could learn.

By focusing on one clear idea and testing it early, we were able to gather useful insights about how interactive learning can influence everyday behaviour.
